coredata.dc:title: Gel-sol and colorimetric dual-modal sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of iodide ions based on gelatin fabricated AuNPs
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yiyun
Bian, Jie
Li, Yuxi
Lin, Tao
Zhang, Jifan
Huo, Kefeng
Liu, Xunyong
Liu, Yanli
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Excessive intake or lack of iodide ions (I-) will have serious impact on human growth and health. Therefore, the highly selective and sensitive detection of I- is particularly urgent. Herein, a dual-modal sensor based on colorimetric and gel-sol signal responses has been developed for the visual detection of I-. Visual sensing based on I- triggered dual-signal simultaneous response can reduce the interference of false positive signals and improve detection accuracy. This dual-modal sensor (Gel-AuNPs) is constructed by the in situ reduction of chloroauric acid using gelatin (Gel) with unique topological structure and reduction properties to prepare gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The presence of I- can induce a noticeable color change from red to yellow (attributed to the occurrence of redox reaction), and the selective visual detection of I- was realized in a wide concentration range with a linear fitting coefficient of 0.9983 and a limit of detection (LOD) of 30.1 μM. Simultaneously, based on the change in viscosity in the gel-sol phase transformation process (attributed to the salting-in effect of I-), the quantitative relationship of I- detection (R 2 = 0.9937) was then established. Furthermore, the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel exhibited high sensitivity for detecting I- in actual water samples such as seawater and tap water. More importantly, the in situ synthesized Gel-AuNPs showed good solid-state stability and strong salt tolerance, implying the potential scope of their practical application in different harsh conditions. As such, this visual sensing system with dual signal responses will have prospective applications in detection and analysis in due course.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-modal
Gel-sol
Colorimetric
I- detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Visual sensing mechanism of Gel-AuNPs for I-
The sensitivity of Gel-AuNPs hydrogel probe
The selectivity of Gel-AuNPs hydrogel probe
Gel-AuNPs hydrogel for I- detection in real water samples and probe stability

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52073134 , 61804089 ), Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation ( ZR2019BB026 , ZR2019MEM026 ) and Yantai Key R&D Program ( 2020XDRH098 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XPS full spectrum of Gel-AuNPs; (b) High-resolution spectra of Au4f; (c) and (d) are transmission electron microscope images of Gel-AuNPs and Gel-AuNPs/I-; (e) and (f) are EDS of Gel-AuNPs and Gel-AuNPs/I-.

Colorimetric response of the probe: (a) color changes built-in image and absorption spectra of (Ⅰ) 86.7 μM Gel-AuNPs with gelatin (ω = 12.5%); (Ⅱ) 86.7 μM Gel-AuNPs with gelatin (ω = 12.5%) in the presence of 93.8 mM H2O2; (Ⅲ) 86.7 μM Gel-AuNPs with gelatin (ω = 12.5%) in the presence of 16.7 mM I-; (Ⅳ) 86.7 μM Gel-AuNPs with gelatin (ω = 12.5%) in the presence of 93.8 mM H2O2 and 16.7 mM I- with a pin to yellow color change; (b) XPS full spectrum of Gel-AuNPs; (c) High-resolution spectra of Au; (d) High resolution spectrum of I.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of 86.7 μM Gel-AuNPs hydrogel with different I- concentration solutions in the presence of H2O2. (b) The relationship between the absorbance of the system at 350 nm and the concentration of I-.

(a) Color and phase changes and (b) viscosity spectra of 86.7 μM Gel-AuNPs hydrogel with different I- concentration solutions in the presence of H2O2. (c) The relationship between the average viscosity of the system and the concentration of I-.

Selectivity of the probe: (a) phase changes; (b) and (c) are the UV–vis absorption spectra of and viscosity change spectra of 86.7 μM Gel-AuNPs hydrogel containing I- (13.9 mM) and several other anions (13.9 mM) in the presence of H2O2, respectively; The embedded graph in the graph b is the response A350 of 86.7 μM Gel-AuNPs hydrogel containing (13.9 mM) and several other anions (13.9 mM) in the presence of H2O2.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of 86.7 μM Gel-AuNPs hydrogel with different I- concentration solutions in the presence of H2O2 in sea water. (b) The relationship between the absorbance of the system at 350 nm and the concentration of I-.

(a) Color and phase changes and (b) viscosity changes of 86.7 μM Gel-AuNPs hydrogel with different I- concentration solutions in the presence of H2O2 in sea water. (c) The relationship between the average viscosity of the system and the concentration of I-.
Preparation of Gel-AuNPs hydrogel and its detection mechanism for I-.

head.ce:title: Gel-sol and colorimetric dual-modal sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of iodide ions based on gelatin fabricated AuNPs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, 264025 Yantai, Shandong Province, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, 264025 Yantai, Shandong Province, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Information and Electronic Engineering, Shandong Technology and Business University, 264005 Yantai, Shandong Province, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Information and Electronic Engineering, Shandong Technology and Business University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Information and Electronic Engineering, Shandong Technology and Business University, 264005 Yantai, Shandong Province, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive intake or lack of iodide ions (I-) will have serious impact on human growth and health. Therefore, the highly selective and sensitive detection of I- is particularly urgent. Herein, a dual-modal sensor based on colorimetric and gel-sol signal responses has been developed for the visual detection of I-. Visual sensing based on I- triggered dual-signal simultaneous response can reduce the interference of false positive signals and improve detection accuracy. This dual-modal sensor (Gel-AuNPs) is constructed by the in situ reduction of chloroauric acid using gelatin (Gel) with unique topological structure and reduction properties to prepare gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The presence of I- can induce a noticeable color change from red to yellow (attributed to the occurrence of redox reaction), and the selective visual detection of I- was realized in a wide concentration range with a linear fitting coefficient of 0.9983 and a limit of detection (LOD) of 30.1 μM. Simultaneously, based on the change in viscosity in the gel-sol phase transformation process (attributed to the salting-in effect of I-), the quantitative relationship of I- detection (R 2 = 0.9937) was then established. Furthermore, the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel exhibited high sensitivity for detecting I- in actual water samples such as seawater and tap water. More importantly, the in situ synthesized Gel-AuNPs showed good solid-state stability and strong salt tolerance, implying the potential scope of their practical application in different harsh conditions. As such, this visual sensing system with dual signal responses will have prospective applications in detection and analysis in due course.
The novel dual-modal readout sensor for I- detection showed high sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Iodine ions (I-) are the important substances that are essential for human growth and development. They play a vital role in many biological processes, such as nerve activity, thyroid and pituitary function [1,2]. The imbalance of I- in the body can lead to abnormal thyroid function. Excessive I- can lead to hyperthyroidism, autoimmune thyroid disease, thyroid cancer and even mental development disorders [3]. The lack of I- can lead to endemic goiter, cretinism, hypothyroidism, decreased fertility, endemic mental retardation, congenital malformations, etc. [4,5]. Therefore, the rapid and accurate detection of I- is extremely important. Traditional I- detection methods include inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [6], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry [7], atomic absorption spectrometry [8], capillary electrophoresis [9], etc. Although these methods have high sensitivity, most of them involve complex multistep sample processing or require precision instruments which makes it difficult to achieve rapid detection in real time.

The visual detection method with the advantages of simple operation, low cost and fast analysis speed provides the possibility for the convenient and rapid detection of I-. The current visual methods for detecting I- include the colorimetric method [10–12], fluorescence method [13–15] and gel-sol method [16]. The gel-sol method is a visual detection method based on phase transition. This method not only endows the sensor to have obvious visible signals, but also enables the sensor to be used by visually impaired people, such as color blind, color weak, or blind, which significantly broadens its users. However, due to the difficulty in designing and constructing I- triggered phase transition systems, the gel-sol method is still rarely used for I- detection. Shen et al. [16] reported that a supramolecular hydrogel containing Ag(I) and glutathione achieved the visual detection of I-. However, the preparation of supramolecular hydrogel is very complicated and time-consuming, and the detection response is very slow, which requires at least an hour. Therefore, it is essential to detect I- based on a hydrogel that is easy to prepare and has a sensitive gel-sol effect. Based on the good salting-in effect of I- on polyvinyl alcohol hydrogels reported by Wu et al. [17], we envisaged whether the gel-sol detection system of I- could be constructed by using the characteristics of gelatin that is easy to gel and the influence of I- on gelatin to achieve the phase transition. If this proposal could be realized, a new strategy for I- detection can be provided.

In addition, most of the current visual sensors only have one visual signal response to the target. The visual sensors which could produce two kinds of signal responses could significantly reduce the interference of false-positive signals, thereby improving detection accuracy. Thus, it is worth constructing a dual-signal probe to detect I-. Currently, except for the gel-sol method, the more common visual detection method is colorimetric detection. The key thing is how to introduce visual colorimetric signal into the gelatin system for I- detection. AuNPs have excellent sensing performance due to their small particle size, large specific surface area and tunable local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [18], so they have been widely used as colorimetric probes. The synthesis of AuNPs mainly utilized the classical citrate reduction method. As the matrix, gelatin itself contains a large number of amino groups that have good reduction properties. Therefore, we intended to prepare AuNPs by in situ reduction of chloroauric acid with gelatin, which can avoid introducing other substances into the gelatin detection system and improve the stability of AuNPs [19]. Moreover, it is well reported that the detection mechanism of AuNP-based sensors is mainly based on the state change of AuNPs from dispersion to aggregation or from aggregation to dispersion, which causes changes in the spectrum and color to realize colorimetric detection. However, these two detection mechanisms involve aggregated AuNPs that are extremely unstable. And many external factors (such as high salt, high temperature, etc.) can also cause the aggregation of AuNPs [20], which will interfere with the accuracy of detection. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop a method based on AuNPs to detect I- without AuNPs aggregation. Inspired by the redox reaction between Au0 and H2O2 in the presence of Cl-, we envisaged whether a colorimetric system that can produce noticeable color and spectral changes without the aggregation of AuNPs can be designed based on the stable chemical reaction between dispersed AuNPs and I-, which is bound to overcome the interference of false-positive signals that cause the aggregation of AuNPs.

Herein, based on the above design ideas, and by utilizing the unique advantages of gelatin (such as multifunctional groups, good reduction property, excellent gelling ability, and gel-sol phase transition characteristics), this work intends to use gelatin as the reducing agent and stabilizer to synthesize AuNPs in situ to solve the problem of poor stability of AuNPs [19,21]. The redox reaction among I-, AuNPs and H2O2 and the salting-in effect of I- on gelatin, can simultaneously cause the color change and phase change of the detection system, thus constructing a dual-signal response visual detection method.

Sodium iodide (NaI) was purchased from Aladdin. Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (AuCl3·HCl·4H2O) was brought from Tianjin Ruijinte Chemical Co., Ltd. Gelatin, hydrogen peroxide and other chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were performed by using a T6 UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Purkinje General, Beijing, China). The size of the nanoparticles was measured by dynamic light scattering on a Malvern Instruments (Malvern, UK) Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 at 25 °C. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) analysis were performed on a Philips (Amsterdam, Netherlands) Talos F200X G2 operating at 200 kV. The surface composition of the nanoparticles was measured by a ESCALAB Xi+ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, USA). The solution viscosity of samples was measured by a Rotated rheometer (DHR-2, USA). High resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) analysis was measured by a Thermo Scientific Q Exactive.

AuNPs were prepared by in situ reduction of chloroauric acid solution with gelatin as reducing reagent. And gelatin also acted as a stabilizer to prevent the aggregation of AuNPs. The preparation process of AuNPs is as follows: Briefly, 78.8 mg of gelatin was added to 40 mL of ultrapure water until gelatin was dissolved at 50 °C (water bath). Subsequently, 4.24 mL of HAuCl4 solution (4.86 mM) was added to 36 mL of ultrapure water, then the mixture was transferred to a three-necked flask. The HAuCl4 solution was heated to reflux, and gelatin solution was added under vigorous stirring. After the solution was deep red, the heating was stopped and stirring was continued for 15 min. The reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and refrigerated for later use.

In order to investigate the detection mechanism of this probe towards I-, with Gel-AuNPs and gelatin solution as the control blank, I- and H2O2 were added to the system respectively or simultaneously. All detection experiments were fixed at a constant volume of 3 mL.

Several key factors affecting the detection including the concentration ratio of H2O2 and I-, the concentration of AuNPs and gelatin were investigated. For the concentration ratio of H2O2 and I-, 0.5 mL Gel-AuNPs was mixed with 0.5 mL gelatin (ω = 10%). And then 0–1.5 mL H2O2 (0.05 M) and 0.5 mL I- solution (4.45 mM) were added to the above solution. Similarly, we changed the concentration of AuNPs from 8.67 μM to 0.13 mM, and the mass concentration of gelatin from 0.5% to 17.5 to optimize the detection conditions. For the pH value, 0.5 mL H2O2 (0.27 M) and 0.5 mL I- solution (0.05 M) were added to 1 mL Gel-AuNPs (0.26 mM) under the pH values from 5 to 11. Similarly, we changed the temperature from 10 to 40 °C, and the salt concentrations from 0 to 10 g/L to optimize the detection conditions.

To investigate the sensitivity of this visual probe, 1 mL of Gel-AuNPs was mixed with 1 mL of gelatin and 0.5 mL of H2O2 (from 0 to 124.7 mM). Then, 0.5 mL of I- solution at different concentrations (from 0 to 22.2 mM) was added to the above solution. The sensitivity of the visual probe for I- was reflected by the change of solution color, UV–vis absorption spectra and solution viscosity. All detection experiments were performed in triplicate. To evaluate the practicability of this visual probe, Gel-AuNPs were used for the detection of I- in seawater and tap water according to the same method and steps mentioned above. In addition, in order to further explore the practicability of the probe in biological samples, standard recovery experiments were carried out in urine and blood samples.

To investigate the selectivity of Gel-AuNPs for I-, 0.5 mL of H2O2 solution (0.47 M) and 0.5 mL of other metal ions (83.4 mM) including I-, F-, Cl-, Br-, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, NO3 -, NO2 -, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, PO4 3-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 - and IO3 - were added to 1 mL of Gel-AuNPs (0.26 mM). The color change, UV–vis absorption spectra and solution viscosity of samples were recorded by digital camera, UV–vis spectrophotometer and Rotated rheometer, respectively.

As shown in Scheme 1, Gel-AuNPs were prepared by in situ reduction using gelatin as the reducing agent and stabilizer. Then, a Gel-AuNPs hydrogel probe was prepared by combining Gel-AuNPs with a gelatin solution. Based on the redox reaction among I-, AuNPs and H2O2 and the salting-in effect of I- on gelatin, a visual sensor with a dual-signal response of I- was constructed.

The elemental composition of the Gel-AuNPs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1a, the Au4f peak can be seen clearly in the full scan spectrum of Gel-AuNPs. In the high-resolution Au4f spectrum (Fig. 1b), the peaks of Au(0)4f7/2 (82.61 eV) and Au(0)4f5/2 (86.24 eV) appeared, which proved that gelatin successfully reduced Au(III) to AuNPs [20]. At the same time, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the nanoparticle size and morphology of the as-synthesized Gel-AuNPs. It can be seen from Fig. 1c that AuNPs had a particle size of approximately 8 nm with a relatively more dispersed spherical or spheroidal structure. In addition, the size of Gel-AuNPs was also determined by DLS analysis. As can be seen from Fig. S1a, the typical dynamic dimension of AuNPs was 13.5 nm, which was slightly larger than that obtained from TEM. This is because DLS measures the hydrodynamic diameter of NPs dispersed in water, which has a looser nanostructure [20,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual sensing mechanism of Gel-AuNPs for I-
ce:section.ce:para:
Scheme 1 shows the proposed I- sensing mechanism of the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel based visual probe. A dual-modal sensor based on colorimetric and gel-sol signal responses was developed for the visual detection of I-. The visual sensing based on an I- triggered dual-signal simultaneous response can reduce the interference of false-positive signals and improve detection accuracy. From curve I Fig. 2a, we can see that the surface plasmon resonance peak of Gel-AuNPs appeared at 520 nm. When H2O2 or I- was added alone, the solution color (image II and III in Fig. 2a) and UV–vis absorption spectra (curve II and III in Fig. 2a) of Gel-AuNPs hardly changed. With the addition of H2O2 and I-, the Gel-AuNPs solution changed from red to yellow. At the same time, not only did the absorption peak of AuNPs at 520 nm disappear, but a new I3 - absorption peak appeared at 350 nm (curve Ⅳ in Fig. 2a). Moreover, it has been reported that Au(0) can be oxidized to Au(I) through a redox reaction in the presence of H2O2 and halide with the color changing from red to yellow [23], which was similar to our detection mechanism. Therefore, we speculate that in the presence of H2O2 and I-, AuNPs (red) were oxidized to Au(I), while I- was oxidized to I3 -, which changed the probe’s color from red to yellow and caused changes in the UV–vis absorption spectra. The entire oxidation reaction can be described as follows:
(1) 2Au0 + 4I-
+ H2O2
⇌ 2AuI2 -
+ H2O
(2) H2O2 + 3I-
+ 2H+
→ I3 -
+ 2H2O

Obviously, if AuNPs are oxidized, the nanoparticles should disappear. Therefore, TEM was performed on the probe system after I- detection. It can be seen from the TEM of Gel-AuNPs/I- that there were no gold nanoparticles in the image (Fig. 1d). Based on the redox reaction, AuNPs formed by zero-valent gold aggregation should be oxidized to dispersed ionic states. In order to verify this state change, EDS analysis was carried out. Pure AuNPs showed that gold elements were highly concentrated particles (Fig. 1e), while after the addition of I-, gold elements were evenly dispersed rather than nanoparticles (Fig. 1f). To confirm our speculation, the elemental composition of Gel-AuNPs/I- was further analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2, in the full scan spectrum of Gel-AuNPs/I-, Au4f and I3d peaks can be seen clearly [24]. In the high-resolution Au4f and I3d spectra of Gel-AuNPs/I- (Fig. 2c and d), Au4f7/2 (85.36 eV), Au4f5/2 (88.97 eV), I3d5/2 (619.08 eV) and I3d3/2 (630.62 eV) peaks appeared respectively, which was consistent with the binding energy of Au(I) and I3 - reported in the literature [25–29]. The above results proved that Au(0) was successfully oxidized to Au(I), while I- was successfully oxidized to I3 -. Furthermore, the presence of I3 - was confirmed by HRMS analysis. As can be seen from Fig. S2, there is an obvious signal at 380.71, which corresponds to the molecular weight of I3 -. For phase change detection, with a large radius and low charge density, I- has a weak hydration ability and can easily interact directly with N atoms in the amide functional group with the opposite charge in the probe, which improves the water solubility of the probe and reduces the viscosity of the probe, thus showing a good salting-in effect. Therefore, the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel probe for the detection of I- can simultaneously produce two signal responses of color change and phase change, which greatly improves the selectivity and accuracy of the probe.

It can be seen from the sensing mechanism that oxidant H2O2 plays an important role in realizing the visual detection of I-. Therefore, we fixed the concentration of I- and changed the concentration of H2O2 to explore the most appropriate ratio of H2O2 and I- in the detection system. As shown in Fig. S3, with increasing H2O2 concentration, the system changed from pink through colorless and pale yellow to yellow. When the concentration of H2O2 reached 16.7 mM, both the color and UV–vis absorption spectra of the system no longer changed significantly with increasing concentration. Therefore, thoroughly carry out the redox reaction, the concentration of H2O2 was set to 25 mM to ensure its large excess; that is, the optimal ratio of H2O2 to I- was 5.6:1. Meanwhile, it is well known that the sensitivity of AuNPs-based sensors is highly dependent on the concentration of AuNPs. Therefore, the visual responses of AuNPs at different concentrations in the presence and absence of I- (4.45 mM) were tested in this study. As shown in Fig. S4, when the concentration of AuNPs was lower than 86.7 μM, the color and UV–vis absorption spectra of the detection system did not change significantly before and after the addition of I-, which was not easy to identify by the naked eye. When the concentration of AuNPs reached 86.7 μM, the color and UV–vis absorption spectra of the AuNPs solution changed obviously after the addition of I-. With the continuous increase in AuNPs concentration, although the visual response of the detection system is more obvious, the higher the probe concentration is, the lower the sensitivity is [30]. Therefore, based on the above results and analysis, 86.7 μM was selected as the optimal concentration for AuNPs to detect I-. In addition, the concentration of gelatin was also studied in detail, because its concentration was a key factor affecting the sensitivity of the gel-sol method. Fig. S5 shows the effect of different gelatin concentrations on the probe system's phase state (qualitative signal response) and viscosity (quantitative signal response). When the mass concentration of gelatin was 12.5%, the probe changed from liquid to solid, and the viscosity also changed sharply (from 1 Pa s to 40 Pa s). A further increase in gelatin concentration will reduce the detection sensitivity of the gel-sol method, so the optimal concentration of gelatin was selected as 12.5% for subsequent experiments. In addition, we optimized the influence of pH, temperature and salt concentrations on the detection system. For pH optimization, the influence of pH value on the stability of the Gel-AuNPs was firstly investigated. As can be seen from Fig. S6, Gel- AuNPs were relatively stable at pH ranging from 5 to 13 after 24 h, but became unstable below pH 4. Therefore, the influence of pH 5–11 on the detection system was investigated. It can be seen from the embedded images of Fig. S7a that when the pH value was 7–11, the color change of Gel-AuNPs/I- system was not obvious or even no color change occurred. It can also be seen from their UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S7a) that there was no obvious change of absorption intensity at 350 nm, resulting in weak signal response. In contrast, when pH was 5–6, the color changed rapidly from red to yellow accompanied by strong absorption at 350 nm. Therefore, weak acid to neutral environment was conducive to the detection of I-. Although there were obvious color change and strong absorption intensity at pH = 5, pH = 5 was the edge pH where AuNPs existed stably. Therefore, pH= 6 was chosen as the optimal pH, and the original detection system without adjusting pH was also 6. For temperature optimization, it can be seen from Fig. S8 that the detection time to reach equilibrium was shortened with the increase of temperature, that was, the increase of temperature was conducive to the occurrence of reaction, which was consistent with the literature [23]. When the temperature changed from 10 to 40 °C, the equilibrium time was 0.5–7 min. Therefore, in order to ensure the reaction can be carried out thoroughly, the detection can be operated at ambient temperature, and the incubation time is controlled to be 10 min. For salt concentrations optimization, experimental results demonstrated that no obvious changes in color (the embedded images of Fig. S7b) and UV–vis spectra (Fig. S7b) under different salt concentrations (0–10 g/L) were observed for Gel-AuNPs/I-, indicating that the detection system was very stable against the variation of salinity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity of Gel-AuNPs hydrogel probe
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the sensitivity of the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel for I- was evaluated. With the increase of I- concentration, the color of the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel changed from red to yellow (Fig. 4a) because AuNPs and I- were oxidized to Au(I) and I3 - in the presence of H2O2, respectively. Meanwhile, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Gel-AuNPs at 520 nm gradually disappeared, and the characteristic absorption peak of I3 - at 350 nm increased with increasing I- concentration ( Fig. 3a). More importantly, the values of A 350 nm (the absorbance value of the system at 350 nm in the presence of I-) increased linearly in the range of I- concentrations from 1.11 μM to 14 mM, and the correlation coefficient was 0.9983 (Fig. 3b). The limit of detection (LOD) for I- calculated using 3σ/K was 30.1 μM. It should be noted that the sensitivity of colorimetric probes is highly dependent on the concentration of AuNPs. Although the detection sensitivity is usually higher with the lower concentration of AuNPs, the linear concentration range is usually narrower. At the same time, when AuNPs concentration is low, the color change of the system is not obvious before and after detection. To this end, the detection of I- by Gel-AuNPs hydrogel at a low concentration (21.7 μM) was compared. The experiment results showed that the values of A350 nm increased linearly in the range of I- concentrations from 0.11 μM to 5.56 mM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9922 (Fig. S9) and a LOD of 3.99 nM.

In addition to the changes in color and UV–vis absorption spectra, the phase state and viscosity of the probe system also changed with the concentration of I-. As shown in Fig. 4, the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel probe gradually changed from solid to liquid with increasing I- concentration, and the viscosity gradually decreased. This is because the increase in the concentration of I-, the salting-in effect of I- increases the solubility of gelatin. The reason why I- has a strong salting-in effect is that I- belongs to a typical chaotropic ion. It has been reported that ions have been classified as either kosmotropes (water-structure makers) or chaotropes (water-structure breakers) according to whether they are strongly hydrated or weakly hydrated with the interaction between waters as a standard [31]. Kosmotropic ions and chaotropic ions usually exhibit the salting-out and salting-in effects, respectively. Interestingly, the average viscosity of η decreased linearly over the I- concentration ranging from 0.11 to 22.2 mM, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9937 (Fig. 4c). The LOD calculated using 3σ/K was 44.4 μM. A comparison of the sensing parameters of other visual methods is given in Table S1. The LOD of our probe for I- ions is comparable or better than those obtained using other visual methods. Based on the above results, the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel probe has a high sensitivity to the dual-signal responses of colorimetry and phase transition, and has good application prospects in I- detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of Gel-AuNPs hydrogel probe
ce:section.ce:para: To study the selectivity of the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel for I- detection, other anions including I-, F-, Cl-, Br-, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, NO3 -, NO2 -, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, PO4 3-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 - and IO3 - were detected. As shown in Fig. 5a and b, except for I-, other anions did not cause changes in the color and phase state of the detection system. Although NO2 - also caused a slight color change from red to light red-brown, the color change caused by NO2 - was significantly different from that caused by I, and no phase change was observed in the detection system. Fig. 5c indicates that the A 350 nm value (colorimetric signal response) could be increased significantly from 1.0 of blank to 3.8 upon the addition of I-. Still, the A 350 nm values for other metal ions were all approximately 1.0–1.5. The viscosity (gel-sol signal response) decreased significantly from 50 Pa s of blank to 9.8 Pa·s after adding I-, while the viscosity of other ions remained at approximately 40–45 Pa s, except for the 35 Pa s of NO2 - (Fig. 5d). The above results indicated that the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel based on dual signal responses had high selectivity for the detection of I-.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gel-AuNPs hydrogel for I- detection in real water samples and probe stability
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the practicability of the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel, real water samples of seawater (
Figs. 6 and 7) and tap water (Figs. S10 and S11) were selected as the verification objects for I- detection. Figs. 6 and 7a show that with increasing I- concentration, the seawater system's color, and UV–vis absorption spectra show roughly the same changes as those of the pure water system. The A 350 nm values also increased linearly within the I- concentration of 0.11–13.9 mM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9947 (Fig. 6c), and the LOD calculated by 3σ/K was 41.2 μM. At the same time, the gel-sol method also showed a good linear detection range and sensitivity in seawater. The average viscosity values decreased linearly with the concentration of I- in the range of 0.11–22.2 mM, and the correlation coefficient and LOD were 0.9901 (Fig. 7c) and 55.6 μM, respectively. Moreover, the detection of I- by the Gel-AuNPs hydrogel in a tap water system was also evaluated. Both the colorimetric and gel-sol methods showed good response performance (Figs. S10 and S11). The above results show that the proposed visual sensor based on dual-signal responses can be used for the high-accuracy detection of I- in real water samples with broad prospects. To further evaluate the precision and accuracy of the developed probe in the biological samples, the detection of I- in urine and blood samples was tested. The urine samples for colorimetric and gel-sol method were spiked with 0.7, 3.0, 7.0 mM as well as 1, 10, 18 mM concentrations of I- (Table S2 and Fig. S12). The found concentrations based on the linear relationship between A350 nm and I- concentration were 0.82, 2.98 and 6.91 mM with recoveries of 98.71–117.14%. At the same time, the found concentrations based on the linear relationship between average viscosity values and I- concentration were 1.28, 9.98 and 18.19 mM with recoveries of 99.80–128.00%. In addition, the blood samples were spiked with the same concentrations of I- as the urine (Table S3 and Fig. S13). The recoveries of colorimetric and gel-sol method were 96.00–99.00% and 98.90–123.00% respectively. The above results indicate that the developed probe has high accuracy for the detection of I- in the biological samples. Moreover, AuNPs-based probes are prone to aggregate under the influence of different environmental factors, resulting in poor selectivity and low accuracy for target detection [32–34]. We further studied the stability of Gel-AuNPs according to the previous method [20]. As can be seen from Figs. S14–S16, Gel-AuNPs have better stability at solid-state and high ionic strength than citrate-capped AuNPs, which lays the foundation for their wide application in the field of ion detection.

Gel-AuNPs were successfully synthesized by in situ reduction of HAuCl4 with gelatin and designed as a dual-signal response visual sensor for I- detection. The colorimetric and gel-sol dual-mode visual sensor with high selectivity and sensitivity could be constructed by adjusting the concentration of AuNPs and gelatin. In the presence of I-, the colorimetric probe showed a noticeable color change from red to yellow, achieving the selective visual detection of I- in a wide concentration range, with an excellent linear fitting coefficient of 0.9983 and a LOD of 30.1 μM. Simultaneously, based on the change in the viscosity of the probe system during the gel-sol phase transition, a quantitative relationship (R 2 = 0.9937) for I- detection was established. Importantly, the colorimetric and gel-sol dual-modal visual sensor could be used for rapid and visual detection of I- in real water samples such as seawater and tap water, and performed a good linear range and high sensitivity. Furthermore, based on the special topological structure of gelatin and the reduction property of its amine group, the in situ synthesized Gel-AuNPs demonstrate good solid-state stability and strong salt tolerance, which can expand the scope of their practical application in different environments. Therefore, the developed colorimetry and phase transition based dual-modal visual sensor might provide a strategy and potential for the highly selective and sensitive determination of practical environmental samples.

Yiyun Zhang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Validation. Jie Bian: Data curation, Resources, Validation. Yuxi Li: Visualization, Investigation. Tao Lin: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jifan Zhang: Software, Validation. Kefeng Huo: Software, Validation. Xunyong Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yanli Liu: Software, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Yi Liu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52073134, 61804089), Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (ZR2019BB026, ZR2019MEM026) and Yantai Key R&D Program (2020XDRH098).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131913.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yiyun Zhang is a graduate student in School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, China. Her major research interest is nanoparticle visual sensor.
Jie Bian is a graduate student in School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, China. His major research is focused on the preparation of nanomaterials.
Yuxi Li is a graduate student in School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, China. His major research interest is the preparation and application of nanosensors.
Tao Lin is a graduate student in School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, China. His current research direction is the application of noble metal nanoparticles.
Jifan Zhang is a graduate student in School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, China. His current research interests are in design and development of optical sensors.
Kefeng Huo is a graduate student in School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, China. His major research is focused on the preparation and application of nanomaterials.

Xunyong Liu is an associate professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, China. He received his M.S. degree in organic chemistry in 2008 and Ph.D. degree in polymer chemistry and physics in 2011 from Tianjin University. His research interests are the developments of chemical sensors and biosensors.

Yanli Liu is a professor in School of Information and Electronic Engineering, Shandong Technology and Business University, China. She received her Ph.D. degree in 2015 from Nanjing University. His research interests are the developments of sensors.

Yi Liu is an associate professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, China. She received her Ph.D. degree in 2013 from Tianjin University. Her research is focused on nanomaterials based chemosensor.
